text: The surgeon uses an adjuvant to establish an entrance and remove adhesions in the abdomen of a female patient. The soft-tongue cystic ovarian is visible, and adhesions are temporarily fixed on the left abdomen wall, followed by temporary fixation of the soft-tongue cyst on the right abdomen wall.

The surgeon uses a Two-O-P-D sewing line to create a bow-shaped suture, revealing the deep structure of the female pelvic basin. The surgical field is visualized with a camera and manipulated using three robotic arms.

The surgeon creates four eight-centimeter openings in the abdominal cavity for the insertion of machine arms. The first opening is inserted, followed by two additional openings on the right side, allowing for connection to the surgical machine.
The surgeon injects two surgical nails and uses a pair of scissors, focusing on improving the placement on the left side. An assistant utilizes the fifth insertion port to aid in the operation.

The surgeon carefully cuts the oblique muscles located above the buttocks, taking care to avoid damaging the main nerves responsible for sexual and reproductive functions. The incision is then extended along the right side of the rectum.

The surgeon performs a ventral mesh rectopexy, entering the rectum from the anterior side using mesh material. The camera pans left and right, showing the sound-to-speech interval marked by an arrow where cutting occurs. Robotic surgery provides a three-dimensional view with magnification, allowing detailed visualization even within the bone marrow. The cutting continues until the muscles at the bottom of the bone marrow are fully exposed. The mesh is sewn onto the muscles on both sides of the bone marrow wall and the front wall of the rectum to stabilize the structure. The bone marrow wall is visible at the bottom left of the screen, shrinking when an electronic device is applied.

The surgeon identifies the position of the rectum in the middle of the body by either cleaning the rectum or having the assistant insert a finger into the rectum to pinpoint the correct area. This step visually confirms that the dissection has reached the bottom of the pelvic rectum.

The surgeon inserts a mesh film into the surgical field, adjusting the robotic arm and tweezers to a sewing machine configuration. The mesh is positioned with its widest part and lower section at the bottom of the pelvic cavity. An OPDES sewing needle is then inserted to secure the mesh in place.

The surgeon places the first stitch on the muscle at the bottom of the pelvic basin on the left side. After completing the stitch, the PDS suture line is carefully managed to prevent entanglement with the scissors.

The assistant breaks and removes the sewing line after the completion of sewing. The surgeon places the third stitch superficially on the philtrum, taking care to avoid penetrating into the rectum. The tip of the arrow indicates the location of the philtrum. A prosthetic incisor is used post-surgery to confirm that the suture has not entered the rectum, ensuring no risk of infection.

The surgeon removes the previous sewing line and needle head, then inserts a new sewing line and needle head. The sewing is performed on the right side of the body at the bottom of the bone basin. The surgeon uses a finger to press on the bone basin to confirm its position, with the arrow indicating the muscle location at the bottom of the basin. The needle is sewn deeply to ensure the bone basin is reinforced.
The surgeon places a deep stitch in the muscle at the bottom of the bone basin to reinforce its strength, ensuring secure attachment.
The surgeon removes the sewing needle and thread from the surgical field after completing the stitching process.

The surgeon positions the third needle on the left side of the bone basin and pulls the webbing upward towards the bottom of the bone. Using a non-dissoluble One Exhibit sewing line, the webbing is sewn on top of the bone's bottom, which protrudes in the middle of the body. The stitch is sewn deeply to ensure the webbing remains secure.

The arrow indicates the lower bone located between two large blood vessels. The surgeon carefully confirms the position of the lower bone to avoid damaging the blood vessels and causing significant bleeding.

The surgeon uses scissors to assist with the SC-Boot sewing line, which is not easy to break. The final stitch is placed on the right side of the bottom bone clip, with the arrow indicating the proximity of the urethra to the sewing line, requiring careful attention to avoid contact.
The video shows the tip of the arrow positioned approximately two centimeters away from the urethra, highlighting the proximity of the surgical site to this anatomical structure.
The surgeon uses scissors to cut the seam line, followed by trimming the excess mesh lining.

The surgeon removes excess film manually and inserts a Two-O PDS sewing line. The seam allowance is used to sew the fat around the left side of the rectum, ensuring direct contact between the rectum and surrounding tissue to enhance resilience. The arrow indicates the location of three main nerves associated with sexual function and fertility. The sewing line is tightened around the fat near the left rectum.

The surgeon inserts a V-Lock 3-0 PDS sewing needle to close the mesh lining at the top. The mesh lining is initially tight but will loosen over time. The sewing line features a small loop that allows movement in one direction, eliminating the need for knot tying. The seam is closed by passing the sewing line through a small hole at the end, ensuring no direct contact with the mesh lining.
The surgeon carefully picks up the seam allowance on the right side, ensuring the needle remains closed while sewing close to the seam allowance before cutting.

The surgeon completes the closure of the mesh lining and cuts the sewing line, removing it from the surgical field. The joint fixed to the left side of the abdominal wall is opened and returned to its original position, followed by the same procedure on the right side to release and reposition the soft tissue.
